label: delphi-r2-p6
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 78,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not (and fairly strongly so) that a court finds both halves of the conjunctive proposition true: the Signal messages were \"public records\" under the City of San Jose framework, and the clerk's city-systems-only search, never reaching out to the three councilmembers despite a request explicitly covering \"any platform or account,\" fell short of the Act's reasonable-search obligation. The strongest single data point is the undisclosed 40 surviving screenshots \u2014 a concrete, known-to-exist responsive record that a proper inquiry would have surfaced.",
  "rationales": [
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 holds a writing relating to conduct of public business, retained by an individual official on a personal account, satisfies Gov. Code \u00a7 6252(e); messages about franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will realistically accept,' and RFP timing are arguably a cleaner fit for that test than San Jose's own mixed personal/official texts.",
    "A majority bloc (three of five councilmembers) discussing deal terms on a pending $180M award goes to the heart of 'conduct of the public's business,' strengthening both the substantive-nexus and the 'retained by' prongs San Jose construed broadly to reach individual custodians acting in their official capacity.",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 6252.5 (added by AB 700, eff. 2022) specifically requires agencies to contact officials and can require a signed declaration when a request could reasonably encompass personal-account communications by elected officials, and Pacific Refuse's request expressly demanded records 'on any platform or account,' making a city-systems-only search facially non-compliant with both that statute and the general 'reasonably calculated' search standard.",
    "The one councilmember's 40 surviving screenshots were known (at least to that member) to exist at the time of the response, so a search that never asked any councilmember anything is close to a per se inadequate search independent of how the broader legal test is resolved.",
    "Countervailing factor: San Jose expressly left open how agencies should structure searches of personal accounts and did not address records already destroyed before a request arrives, so a court could still find the messages were public records while treating the 'no responsive records' answer as a closer, more fact-bound question rather than an automatic statutory violation \u2014 this tempers full confidence in the conjunctive proposition as stated."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 6252(e) (public records definition; note 2023 CPRA recodification renumbered many sections into the 7920.000\u20137931.000 series \u2014 verify current citation)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 6252.5, added by AB 700 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456) \u2014 personal-account search/declaration duty for elected officials (verify current recodified section number)",
    "American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 \u2014 'reasonably calculated' search standard",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 34090 \u2014 local agency records retention/destruction authority, relevant to the separate deletion/retention-duty question"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court will treat the 'no responsive records' answer as a clean, per se Act violation (given the explicit request language and the known screenshots) or instead as a fact-bound adequacy-of-search question on which the city could still partially prevail by showing some good-faith limitation, especially since San Jose and its progeny have not squarely resolved search-methodology standards for already-largely-destroyed ephemeral personal-app messages."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 76.9, std dev 3.2, range 70.0–80.0, middle 50% of panel between 76.0 and 80.0.

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Content-controls-not-location (San Jose prong one).** *7 of 7 panelists.* Every panelist grounded the "public record" determination in *City of San Jose*'s content-based test, and every panelist characterized the subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) as core conduct of the public's business.

**Theme B — Duty to inquire of individual custodians / reasonable-search standard.** *7 of 7 panelists.* All invoked some version of a reasonably-calculated or reasonable-diligence search standard and noted the clerk's failure to ask any of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications. Panelists differed on the doctrinal source (San Jose remand instructions, § 6253.1/§ 7922.600 duty-to-assist, AB 700, case law on search adequacy) — see Theme E and Disagreement point 2.

**Theme C — "Any platform or account" request language as notice.** *5 of 7 panelists* (probabilities 70, 76, 78, 78, 80). These panelists argued the request's express wording put the clerk on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, with some describing the resulting search as "facially inadequate" or a candidate for a per-se violation.

**Theme D — The ~40 surviving screenshots.** *7 of 7 panelists, but used in two distinct ways:*
- *6 panelists* (76 through 80) treated the screenshots as making "no responsive records" factually inaccurate at the moment it was given — an independent basis for a violation regardless of the deleted messages.
- *1 panelist* (70) used the screenshots differently: as evidentiary proof of content and participation that allows a court to reach a definitive holding on the public-record question, rather than as proof of response falsity.

**Theme E — AB 700 statutory codification of personal-account search duties.** *2 of 7 panelists* (76, 78) cited AB 700 as codifying an affirmative duty to route requests to individual officials — though with different section numbers and chapter citations (see Disagreement point 5).

**Theme F — Conjunctive fragility of the proposition.** *5 of 7 panelists* (70, 76, 78, 78, 80) expressly noted that the proposition requires both prongs to hold, making it more fragile than either prong alone; the second (search-adequacy) prong was uniformly identified as the weaker link among these panelists.

**Theme G — Clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense.** *3 of 7 panelists* (76, 76, 78) — a mid-range cluster — flagged that a court could excuse the initial search as reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of response.

**Theme H — Majority-bloc participation strengthens the public-business nexus.** *3 of 7 panelists* (78, 80, 80) — skewing toward higher-probability panelists — emphasized that three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) participating in substantive deliberation reinforces both the content nexus and the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element.

**Theme I — Deleted-messages scope problem.** *2 of 7 panelists* (both at 80 — the highest-probability panelists) raised the possibility that a court might limit its "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of auto-deleted messages as a retention/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA determination.

**Theme J — Availability of declaratory relief despite destruction.** *1 panelist* (76) noted that CPRA declaratory/injunctive relief does not require that responsive records presently exist.

**Theme K — Possible exemptions even if records qualify.** *1 panelist* (76) noted content could be found preliminary/deliberative and exempt from disclosure despite public-record status.

**Theme L — Third-party/consultant-hosted platform distinction.** *1 panelist* (70 — the lowest-probability panelist) flagged that San Jose involved the official's own texts/emails, whereas this involves a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform, with no published California decision on point.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials.** Some panelists treat the request's "any platform or account" language as itself sufficient to trigger the duty (making the city-systems-only search per se or facially inadequate); others posit that absent actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group, the search could be found reasonable "at the time." Several panelists hold both positions in tension within their own answers, but as between panelists the trigger assumption is incompatible: request-language-alone versus knowledge-required.

2. **The state of post-San Jose search-methodology doctrine.** The 70 panelist cites the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision (*City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013) as published authority contemplating that agencies must ask custodians to search personal accounts. Other panelists (78, 78, 80) describe search methodology as expressly left unresolved by San Jose, with no binding appellate authority fixing the duty's contours. These are incompatible readings of the same doctrinal landscape.

3. **Scope of any "public records" holding as to the deleted messages.** The two 80 panelists assume a court might confine its holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and recharacterize the rest as a retention/spoliation matter; other panelists' analyses assume the full body of Signal messages is subject to the public-record determination.

4. **Whether the Signal platform's characteristics matter.** The 70 panelist treats the consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, ephemeral nature of the channel as a genuine open question under the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element; other panelists (78, 80) describe these facts as a *cleaner* fit for San Jose than San Jose's own facts, treating the platform's characteristics as immaterial.

5. **What Gov. Code § 7922.545 is.** One panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the AB 700 personal-account search provision (Stats. 2021, ch. 132); another panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the recodified preliminary-drafts exemption (former § 6254(a)); a third panelist (78) cites the AB 700 provision as § 6252.5 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456). These citations are mutually inconsistent as to section number and chapter, and multiple panelists flagged their own citations for verification.

6. **Per se violation versus fact-bound adequacy question.** Panelists split on whether the "no responsive records" response would be treated as a clean statutory violation or as a fact-intensive search-adequacy question on which the city could partially prevail through a good-faith showing.

## 4. Authorities Cited

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction) — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of "public records") — **4 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — **3 panelists**
- *ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court* (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy) — **3 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253 / § 7922.500 / § 7922.530 (duty to make records available) — **3 panelists**
- AB 700 personal-account search provisions (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5) — **2 panelists** (with inconsistent section/chapter citations; see Disagreement point 5)
- CPRA judicial-enforcement provisions (§ 7923.100, cited variously as former § 6259 and former § 6258) — **2 panelists**

**Cited by one panelist each:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision)
- *Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385
- *Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court* (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (flagged by the citing panelist as an analog to verify)
- Gov. Code § 7920.545 / former § 6252(g) (definition of "writing")
- Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (cited as preliminary drafts/notes exemption)
- Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting)
- Gov. Code § 7920.000 et seq. (general CPRA recodification citation)

Several panelists flagged their own recodified section numbers for verification; those flags are preserved above without correction.

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

The consultant-platform distinction (Theme L) and deleted-messages-scope point (Theme I) were already implicitly within my round-1 uncertainty band and don't change the calculus on reflection. I held at 78 rather than drift toward the panel median/mean by default, per the instruction not to defer without a validated reason."}

data.position_summary: Unchanged from round 1: more likely than not, and fairly strongly so, that a court holds both halves of the conjunctive proposition true — the Signal messages were "public records" under San Jose's content-based test, and the clerk's city-systems-only search (never contacting the three councilmembers despite a request explicitly reaching "any platform or account") violated the CPRA's reasonable-search obligation, independently confirmed by the known-but-undisclosed 40 surviving screenshots.
rationales: The panel's near-unanimous convergence (7/7) on the content-controls-not-location threshold and (6/7) on the screenshots-as-proof-of-falsity theory tracks my own reasoning exactly and reinforces rather than revises it — this is confirmatory, not new information, so it doesn't independently justify moving the number.

The 'clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge' defense (Theme G, 3/7) is a genuine countervailing consideration I take seriously, but I judge it doctrinally weaker than it first appears: the entire post-San Jose/AB 700 inquiry duty exists precisely because agencies often lack advance knowledge of personal-account communications — the trigger is the scope of the request ('any platform or account'), not the clerk's prior awareness of a specific Signal group. I don't think this defense, standing alone, should pull the probability down materially.

The doctrinal-state disagreement (point 2: what the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision, 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, actually holds about search methodology) is a real source of added uncertainty I did not weigh explicitly in round 1 — if that decision does mandate custodian-level searches, it strengthens the second conjunct; if search methodology remains genuinely unresolved, the fact-bound framing (which I already flagged as my top uncertainty) gets more weight. I treat this as roughly a wash against the confirmatory themes above.

The third-party/consultant-hosted, ephemeral-by-design platform issue (Theme L, the panel's lowest scorer) remains a legitimate open question with no on-point published CA authority, but I still think it's a weaker distinction than San Jose's own facts suggest — the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' element focuses on the official's relationship to the writing, not who built the app, so I don't discount for this beyond what I already built into my confidence level.

The deleted-messages-scope point (Theme I, the two highest scorers) doesn't move me either direction: even if a court confines its itemized public-record findings to the surviving screenshots, the proposition as worded is a categorical statement about 'the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise' that a court could satisfy through a general legal holding about the class of communications, without needing message-by-message proof for the deleted portion.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 — remand decision on search methodology (verify actual holding; panel disagreed on its scope)
Gov. Code § 7920.530 / former § 6252(e) (definition of public records)

Gov. Code provisions on personal-account search duty for elected officials, AB 700 (Stats. 2021) — section number disputed among panelists (variously cited as § 6252.5 and § 7922.545); verify correct recodified cite
ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 — reasonably-calculated-search standard
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court treats the "no responsive records" answer as a clean, near-per-se violation (given explicit request language plus the known screenshots) or as a fact-bound search-adequacy question where the city could partially prevail on a good-faith/no-actual-knowledge theory — this turns on unsettled doctrine (including what the 2018 San Jose remand decision actually requires of custodian-level searches) that the panel itself could not agree on.
data.response_to_panel: No single panel theme independently justified a move. The strongest candidates for revision — the knowledge-required defense (Theme G) and the doctrinal split over the 2018 remand decision's scope (Disagreement 2) — are real, but I judge them roughly offset by the near-unanimous confirmatory themes (A, D) that validate my existing reasoning rather than add new grounds. The consultant-platform distinction (Theme L) and deleted-messages-scope point (Theme I) were already implicitly within my round-1 uncertainty band and don't change the calculus on reflection. I held at 78 rather than drift toward the panel median/mean by default, per the instruction not to defer without a validated reason.
